methodology_note: Iter-1 ran two attempts both *with* the skill loaded (A vs B), which measured intra-skill variance, not skill value. Iter-2 corrects this: one attempt with the full edited SKILL.md available, one baseline attempt simulating a Claude session with no testing skill loaded (general PG-internals knowledge only, no access to the decision tree / wiring table / fast-loop commands).

assertions.text: Picks regress (not TAP / isolation / module) as the flavor.
Names the three required files: sql/<name>.sql, expected/<name>.out, parallel_schedule.
assertions.baseline_note: Names sql/ and expected/ correctly but hedges on schedule file ('serial_schedule or parallel_schedule').

assertions.text: Tells the user to GENERATE expected/.out by running and copying from results/, not type by hand.
Mentions the max-20-per-group rule in parallel_schedule.
assertions.baseline_note: Baseline has no awareness of this house rule without the skill.

assertions.text: Warns about volatile output (OIDs, timestamps, row order) before committing.
Provides a single-test fast-loop command (pg_regress or meson).
Points to regression.diffs as first stop on failure.
Picks TAP and places it under source/src/test/recovery/t/NNN_name.pl.
Mentions appending NNN (don't renumber existing tests) for backport safety.
assertions.baseline_note: Mentions NNN ordering but not the don't-renumber / backport rationale.

assertions.text: Mentions wiring into recovery/meson.build.
assertions.baseline_note: Hand-waves: 'make sure the meson build / Makefile picks it up' — doesn't name recovery/meson.build.

assertions.text: Provides a working skeleton with PostgreSQL::Test::Cluster, init(allows_streaming=>1), init_from_backup, start.
Mentions wait_for_replay_catchup (or equivalent sync) as the way to wait for the standby.
assertions.baseline_note: Names it but hedges ('I'd check Cluster.pm for the exact name').

assertions.text: Gives the one-line single-TAP-test runner (meson test --suite recovery NNN_name or PROVE_TESTS=).
Points to build/testrun/<suite>/<test>/log/regress_log_* as first place to look on failure.
assertions.baseline_note: Vague: 'tmp_check/testrun tree' with no concrete path.

assertions.text: Honestly notes that there is no direct 'assert WAL record of type X applied' API — verify via observable state/LSN.
Picks isolation spec, not TAP, for two-session heavyweight-lock contention.
Names the three files: specs/<name>.spec, expected/<name>.out, isolation_schedule.
assertions.baseline_note: Names specs/ and expected/ but hedges on schedule ('I believe').

assertions.text: Calls out that explicit permutation lines are MANDATORY when steps block (else auto-permutations hang).
Mentions isolationtester only detects heavyweight locks via pg_locks (LWLock/buffer pin invisible → injection points).
assertions.baseline_note: Baseline mentions pg_locks polling but misses the LWLock/buffer-pin invisibility and the injection_points escape hatch.

assertions.text: Provides at least a sketch of session/step/permutation grammar.
Discourages _1.out variants; recommends markers (s1(*), s1(s2), s1(s2 notices N)) for stabilization.
assertions.baseline_note: Baseline has no awareness of markers vs _1.out variants — this is PG-house-rule knowledge.

assertions.text: Provides the single-spec runner command (pg_isolation_regress) and points to output_iso/regression.diffs on failure.
assertions.baseline_note: Names pg_isolation_regress but missing --temp-instance / --top-builddir flags; on-failure note is vague ('probably under output_iso/').

notes: Skill provides a real, measurable lift of 6 assertions across the 3 evals. Where the skill wins are exactly the PG-specific house rules a generalist would not know: max-20-per-parallel_schedule-group (eval 1), explicit recovery/meson.build wiring + concrete log path (eval 2), and the LWLock/injection-points caveat plus markers-vs-_1.out idiom (eval 3). The grammar skeleton and LSN-sync recipe added in iter-2 edits both fire in the with_skill answers (eval 3 reproduces the full spec verbatim, eval 2 inlines the LSN poll_query_until form). The 6-point baseline gap is honestly graded — baseline answers are still mostly correct because PG testing patterns are well-known, but the skill consistently sharpens precision (exact file paths, exact flags, exact log location) and surfaces non-obvious gotchas.
